title: Māṇḍūkya-Upaniṣadkārikā [Gauḍapādakārikā, Māṇḍūkyakārikā, Āgamaśāstra]
author: Gauḍapāda
date_estimate: c. 6th-7th c. CE (disputed; must predate Bhāvaviveka, before c. 630 CE)
summary: A foundational Advaita Vedānta text of 215 verses (kārikās) organized into four chapters (prakaraṇas): Āgama, Vaitathya, Advaita, and Alātaśānti. Expounding the non-duality of Ātman/Brahman, it analyzes the four states of consciousness (waking, dream, deep sleep, and turīya) keyed to the syllable Oṃ, and argues for ajātivāda, the doctrine of non-origination, denying that the world is ever truly created.
history: Traditionally ascribed to Gauḍapāda, the paramaguru (teacher's teacher) of Śaṅkara, though his identity and exact date are uncertain. The work, also called the Āgamaśāstra, is celebrated as the earliest systematic exposition of Advaita and is noted for its conspicuous engagement with and absorption of Buddhist (especially Madhyamaka/Yogācāra) terminology and argument; the first chapter circulates intertwined with the Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad and Śaṅkara wrote a commentary on it.

title: Īśa-Upaniṣad [Īśāvāsyopaniṣad]
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 5th-4th c. BCE or later; among the principal Upaniṣads but dating disputed
summary: One of the shortest principal Upaniṣads, comprising 17-18 verses, forming the closing (fortieth) chapter of the Vājasaneyi Saṃhitā of the Śukla (White) Yajurveda. It teaches that the whole world is pervaded by and to be enveloped in the Lord (īśa), urging renunciation-in-action, the harmonizing of knowledge and ritual works, and a vision of the unity of the Self with all beings.
history: Anonymous and traditionally regarded as revealed (śruti); its placement within the Saṃhitā itself (rather than a Brāhmaṇa or Āraṇyaka) is unusual. It is a much-commented text, with bhāṣyas by Śaṅkara and later Vedāntins, and famous opening invocations; two recensions (Kāṇva and Mādhyandina) survive with minor differences.

title: Īśa-Upaniṣad [Īśāvāsyopaniṣad]
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 5th-4th c. BCE or later; dating disputed
summary: The Īśā (Īśāvāsya) Upaniṣad, the brief closing chapter of the Vājasaneyi Saṃhitā of the Śukla Yajurveda, teaching that all is pervaded by the Lord and counseling the reconciliation of action and knowledge for liberation. This record appears to be a second copy or a recension/edition (the 'sb' suffix may indicate an associated commentary, e.g. a bhāṣya, but this is uncertain).
history: Same text as GV05isup___u; this is a distinct catalogue entry, plausibly a different recension (Kāṇva vs. Mādhyandina) or a version accompanied by a commentary such as Śaṅkara's bhāṣya. Heavily commented in the Vedānta tradition.

author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: unknown (likely an Upaniṣad; cannot identify precisely)
summary: No title is supplied in the source metadata. The work_id pattern (GV05isv...) parallels the neighbouring Īśa-Upaniṣad entries (GV05isup...), so this is most plausibly another copy or recension of the Īśā(vāsya) Upaniṣad, but the identification cannot be confirmed from the available data.
history: Identity uncertain owing to the missing title; if it is the Īśā Upaniṣad it shares that text's anonymous śruti status and Vedānta commentarial tradition.

title: Kaivalya-Upaniṣad
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: late 1st millennium BCE, c. 500-200 BCE
summary: A minor Upaniṣad of 24-26 verses teaching the path to kaivalya (isolation, liberation) through renunciation, faith, devotion and meditation. It identifies the meditator's Self with Śiva/Maheśvara, presenting Rudra-Śiva as the supreme Brahman and Ātman, and famously declares 'I am Brahman' while affirming the unity of the individual self with the divine.
history: Anonymous; classified as a Śaiva Upaniṣad and survives in two recensions, one attached to the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda and one to the Atharvaveda. It is dated relatively late among the early Upaniṣads, composed close in spirit to the Śvetāśvatara, Muṇḍaka and Mahānārāyaṇa Upaniṣads.

title: Māṇḍūkya-Upaniṣad
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: disputed; c. 1st-2nd c. CE or earlier (some place it in the early common era / late Upaniṣadic period)
summary: The shortest of the principal Upaniṣads, only twelve prose verses, associated with the Atharvaveda. It analyzes the sacred syllable Oṃ (A-U-M) and correlates its parts with the four states of the Self: waking (vaiśvānara), dreaming (taijasa), deep sleep (prājña), and the transcendent fourth (turīya), which is pure non-dual consciousness.
history: Anonymous śruti; despite its brevity it became immensely influential through Gauḍapāda's Kārikā and Śaṅkara's commentary, and the Muktikā canon ranks it as alone sufficient for liberation. Its terse formulation of the four states is foundational for Advaita Vedānta.

title: Māṇḍūkya-Upaniṣad
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: disputed; c. 1st-2nd c. CE or earlier
summary: A second catalogue copy/recension of the Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad, the twelve-verse Atharvaveda Upaniṣad on Oṃ and the four states of consciousness (waking, dream, deep sleep, turīya). Content is identical to GV05mand_upu.
history: Anonymous śruti; a duplicate or variant edition of the Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad. Famed through Gauḍapāda's Kārikā and Śaṅkara's bhāṣya and central to Advaita Vedānta.

title: Nādabindu-Upaniṣad
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: disputed; c. 100 BCE - 300 CE (Flood) to early 1st millennium CE (Feuerstein)
summary: A minor Yoga Upaniṣad and an early manual of nāda-yoga (the yoga of inner mystic sound). It treats meditation on the praṇava Oṃ, the symbolism of the haṃsa (swan) and its body-parts, and the audition of the unstruck inner sound (anāhata nāda), through which the yogin's mind is absorbed and liberation (mokṣa) attained.
history: Anonymous; attached in traditional listings to the Ṛgveda (some lists to the Atharvaveda) and grouped among the twenty Yoga Upaniṣads. It is among the earliest documents on the yoga of sacred sound, influential on later haṭha- and nāda-yoga traditions.

title: Praśna-Upaniṣad
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 5th-4th c. BCE or later; among the principal/mukhya Upaniṣads
summary: A principal prose Upaniṣad of the Atharvaveda structured as six questions (praśnas) put by six pupils to the sage Pippalāda. It addresses the origin of creatures from prāṇa and rayi (life and matter), the supremacy of prāṇa (breath/vital force), the sixteen parts (kalās) of the person, the states of sleep and dream, and meditation on Oṃ as the way to the supreme Puruṣa.
history: Anonymous śruti, attached to the Atharvaveda and associated with the Pippalāda school. One of the principal Upaniṣads commented on by Śaṅkara; its question-and-answer format makes it a systematic complement to the closely related Muṇḍaka Upaniṣad.

title: Praśna-Upaniṣad
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 5th-4th c. BCE or later
summary: A second catalogue copy of the Praśna Upaniṣad of the Atharvaveda, the six-question dialogue with the sage Pippalāda on prāṇa, creation, the sixteen kalās, sleep, and meditation on Oṃ. The 'b' suffix likely indicates an edition accompanied by a bhāṣya (commentary, e.g. Śaṅkara's).
history: Anonymous śruti; identical text to GV05prasup_u, distinct catalogue entry plausibly bundled with a commentary. A principal Upaniṣad of the Pippalāda Atharvaveda tradition.

title: Śira-Upaniṣad [Atharvaśira-Upaniṣad]
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: disputed; some scholars c. 5th c. BCE (predating the Dharmasūtras), others later 1st millennium BCE/CE
summary: The Atharvaśiras ('head of the Atharva') Upaniṣad, a minor Śaiva Upaniṣad of the Atharvaveda centered on the god Rudra. It proclaims that all gods, all beings and all reality are Rudra, who is the innermost essence and highest goal of everything; it also expounds the Paśupata-vrata and the significance of the syllable Oṃ.
history: Anonymous; one of the cluster of five 'Śiva = Ātman' Upaniṣads and an important early Śaiva/Pāśupata scripture, cited in later ritual and Paśupata literature. Its dating is debated, with some scholars arguing it predates the Dharmasūtras (placing it early), others assigning it a later date.

title: Śvetāśvatara-Upaniṣad
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to the sage Śvetāśvatara)
date_estimate: c. 4th-2nd c. BCE (last centuries BCE); among the later principal Upaniṣads
summary: A principal verse Upaniṣad of the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda, notable as the most theistic of the early Upaniṣads. It develops a personal theology of Rudra-Śiva as the one supreme Lord (Īśa, Deva, Hara), integrating concepts of brahman, sāṃkhya categories, māyā, devotion (bhakti) and yoga, and meditating on the cause of the cosmos.
history: Traditionally ascribed to the sage Śvetāśvatara but effectively anonymous. It is a pivotal bridge between the older Vedāntic Upaniṣads and later devotional, Sāṃkhya-Yoga and Śaiva thought, and contains one of the earliest clear statements of monotheistic bhakti; much commented, including by Śaṅkara's school.

title: Śvetāśvatara-Upaniṣad
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Śvetāśvatara)
date_estimate: c. 4th-2nd c. BCE
summary: A second catalogue copy/recension of the Śvetāśvatara Upaniṣad, the theistic Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda Upaniṣad presenting Rudra-Śiva as the supreme Lord and weaving together brahman, sāṃkhya, māyā, yoga and bhakti. Content matches GV05svetu_pu.
history: Anonymous, ascribed to the sage Śvetāśvatara; a distinct catalogue entry (possibly a different recension or commentary-bearing edition) of this seminal theistic Upaniṣad.

title: Pāraskara-Gṛhyasūtra
author: Pāraskara (traditional)
date_estimate: c. 6th-4th c. BCE (later Vedic / sūtra period)
summary: The principal domestic-ritual manual (gṛhyasūtra) of the Śukla (White) Yajurveda tradition. In aphoristic sūtra style it prescribes the household sacrifices and life-cycle rites (saṃskāras): the daily fire, marriage, conception, birth, name-giving, upanayana (initiation), Vedic study, the new- and full-moon offerings, and funerary observances.
history: Attributed to Pāraskara and forming an appendix to Kātyāyana's Śrautasūtra of the Vājasaneyi (Mādhyandina) school. It is one of the most important and widely used gṛhyasūtras, drawing its mantras from the Vājasaneyi Saṃhitā, and was edited and translated early by Western Indologists (e.g. Stenzler, Oldenberg).

title: Āpastamba-Śulbasūtra
author: Āpastamba (traditional)
tradition: Hindu (Vedic ritual / mathematics), Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda (Taittirīya school)
date_estimate: c. 600-300 BCE (later Vedic period)
summary: A śulba ('cord/measurement') sūtra belonging to the Āpastamba Kalpasūtra of the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda, giving the geometric rules for laying out and constructing Vedic fire-altars (vedi, citi) of prescribed shapes and areas. It contains early geometry including statements equivalent to the Pythagorean theorem and a remarkably accurate rational approximation of the square root of 2.
history: Part of the Āpastamba school's ritual corpus of the Taittirīya (Black Yajurveda). Together with the Baudhāyana, Mānava and Kātyāyana Śulbasūtras it constitutes the oldest extant geometric literature of India, prized in the history of mathematics for its altar geometry and irrational-number approximations.

title: Āśvalāyana-Gṛhyasūtra
author: Āśvalāyana (traditional)
date_estimate: c. 6th-4th c. BCE (later Vedic / sūtra period; Oldenberg)
summary: The domestic-ritual manual of the Ṛgvedic Āśvalāyana school, prescribing in sūtra form the household sacrifices and life-cycle rites (saṃskāras): marriage, the daily and seasonal offerings, upanayana and Vedic study, conception-to-funeral observances, and rites for prosperity. Its mantras are drawn primarily from the Ṛgveda.
history: Attributed to Āśvalāyana, a pupil of Śaunaka, and belonging to the Āśvalāyana Kalpasūtra of the Ṛgveda; it pairs with the Āśvalāyana Śrautasūtra. It is one of the two principal Ṛgvedic gṛhyasūtras (alongside Śāṅkhāyana) and was a key text in early Western Vedic scholarship (the 'not_before' hint 'Oldenberg' refers to Hermann Oldenberg, its translator).

title: Āśvalāyana-Śrautasūtra
author: Āśvalāyana (traditional)
date_estimate: c. 6th-4th c. BCE (later Vedic / sūtra period)
summary: The solemn-sacrifice manual of the Ṛgvedic Āśvalāyana school, codifying in sūtra form the procedures of the great śrauta rituals performed with three fires: the agnihotra, new- and full-moon sacrifices, soma sacrifices, animal offerings and the duties of the hotṛ priest who recites the Ṛgveda.
history: Attributed to Āśvalāyana (pupil of Śaunaka) and forming, with the Āśvalāyana Gṛhyasūtra, the Kalpasūtra of the Śākala Ṛgveda. It is closely related to the Aitareya Brāhmaṇa and serves as a primary practical guide to Ṛgvedic priestly performance.

title: Atharvavedapariśiṣṭas
author: anonymous/traditional (composite)
date_estimate: composite; tracts ranging across the later Vedic and post-Vedic periods (mid-1st millennium BCE into the early common era)
summary: A large collection of about 70-74 supplementary tracts (pariśiṣṭas) appended to the Atharvaveda, treating details not covered in the core texts. They cover royal and priestly rituals, the duties of the purohita, omens and portents, astrology and astronomy, propitiatory and magical rites, and other applied liturgical matter.
history: Anonymous and composite, the Atharvaveda's pariśiṣṭa literature being by far the most extensive among the Vedas. Belonging to different chronological strata, the texts are difficult to date precisely; they are valuable witnesses to popular religion, statecraft and early jyotiṣa associated with the Atharvan tradition.

title: Atharvaprāyaścittāni
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: composite, later Vedic to post-Vedic period (mid-1st millennium BCE onward)
summary: An Atharvavedic text on prāyaścittas, expiatory and atonement rites used to remove sin, ritual error, defilement and inauspiciousness. It prescribes the spells and procedures for expiating faults in sacrifice and in daily life, in keeping with the Atharvaveda's strong concern with apotropaic and corrective magic.
history: Anonymous; one of the ancillary works (counted among or alongside the Atharvaveda pariśiṣṭas) cited within the Atharvan ancillary literature. Like the broader pariśiṣṭa corpus its precise date is hard to fix, reflecting accreted ritual practice of the Atharvan schools.

title: Bhāradvāja-Śrautasūtra
author: Bhāradvāja (traditional)
date_estimate: c. 6th-4th c. BCE (later Vedic / sūtra period)
summary: A solemn-sacrifice (śrauta) manual of the Taittirīya branch of the Kṛṣṇa (Black) Yajurveda, codifying in sūtra form the great fire-rituals: the establishment of the sacred fires, agnihotra, new- and full-moon offerings, soma sacrifices and animal oblations, along with the priestly procedures of the adhvaryu.
history: Attributed to the Bhāradvāja school, one of the seven Śrautasūtra traditions of the Taittirīya Śākhā and reckoned among the earliest sūtrakāras (close to Baudhāyana and preceding/parallel to Āpastamba). It belongs to the Bhāradvāja Kalpasūtra corpus of the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda. (The metadata 'not_after: 0' merely flags a BCE composition.)
